name: Avenc de s'Esquerda
coordinates_utm: DATUM EUROPEAN 1950: 461006 E, 4378133 N. DATUM WGS84: 460904 E, 4377933 N
equipment: North entrance: 50 m rope, 1 bolt for installation, 2 slings or cords (one for a carabiner next to the bolt, another for the rebelay on the ledge), plus a 40+ m rope. South entrance: 50 m rope, 1 bolt for installation and another for a rebelay
description: A spectacular 65 m deep chasm discovered in 2008 in the Serra de Na Burguesa, with two entrances (North and South). The main chamber reaches 50 m in height and is richly decorated with speleothems from floor to ceiling, with a large bat colony. Notable finds include remains of Myotragus balearicus, and a striking stalagmite that grew both vertically and horizontally from a small base, creating one of the most beautiful formations seen.
full_description:
Story of a discovery.

On the morning of February 9, 2008, having nothing to do until lunchtime and finding myself bored, the idea came to me of going for a walk through the Serra de Na Burguesa to try to locate some cavity in an area near the Cova del Tuputut, since according to a member of a forestry brigade who had been working in the area, he had apparently discovered one or another hole in the ground, communicating this to his brother and in turn his brother to me. Before reaching the indicated area, I left the path and had the luck of finding a small chasm that J. Encinas subsequently confirmed was uncatalogued, so it had already been worth going out that day. Once I had taken the coordinates of the cave, which later came to be called "Pouet Escollerat de l'Ase," I continued my way to the area where supposedly I would have no great difficulty finding some of the holes seen by the forestry brigade. What would be my surprise and displeasure when, after combing the area, I had to leave without finding a trace of any of them. On my way home, I passed again in front of the small chasm found a few hours earlier, cursing my bad luck for not having found anything despite the directions. Again I decided to continue my return off the path, though not far from it, while at the same time the idea was going through my head of the possibility that a stroke of luck might allow me to find something before definitively rejoining the path. But suddenly, when I no longer harbored any hope, I thought I observed a depression in the terrain, so I immediately approached the place and could make out a crack in the ground about six meters long by about two meters wide. Without knowing what was hidden inside, my expression already changed, going from being a little angry with myself for not having been able to find anything in the indicated area to being happy regardless of what the crack might hold inside — which perhaps would be nothing special since it was at a straight-line distance of approximately one hundred meters from the path and I had no news that anything important was located in that place.

Back at my home, I contacted J. A. Encinas so he could confirm whether he had any knowledge of the existence of any cave at the coordinates I had taken with my GPS, and he later confirmed, once he had checked his maps, that nothing was catalogued in that place, so we agreed to go take a look as soon as possible.

First exploration.

On March 21, 2008, five of us finally headed to the place with the intention of exploring the crack in question, so we installed a bolt for the rope placement as well as a new safety installation on a natural anchor in the rock, and J. A. Encinas, G. Herraez, and yours truly, J. Bermejo, entered its interior. During the descent through the crack, partly covered by rocks of all sizes fallen from the outside, and while descending I did not harbor great hopes regarding the final result since throughout the entire route everything was more of the same, until everything changed when I finally "landed" on a pile of debris from which I could observe with some clarity what awaited us. After a superficial exploration and assessing the importance of the find, we decided to exit to the outside to return another day and thus finish the exploration while also producing the survey of the cave.

The remains of a hut are found near the south entrance of the chasm, which was surely built in that exact spot and no other in order to take advantage of the thermals from the chasm and heat the hut naturally on cold days. If we look carefully at the surroundings of the hut, it is also possible to see the beginning of the flowstones.

Second exploration

On April 13, 2008, we decided to finish the exploration of the chasm, so again the same three people who had accessed it the first time gathered to do so, beginning by making a new installation at the second entrance or South entrance, which M. L. Redondo found by chance when moving some rocks on the first day of exploration while waiting for us to come out. This second entrance is located a few meters to the south following the crack, with dimensions of 0.40 x 0.50 m.

As no natural anchor was found near the entrance, a bolt had to be installed, just as at what we shall call the main entrance or North entrance, where it is also necessary to install a rebelay at about ten meters depth on a natural anchor that J. A. Encinas found there. If we cannot find it, then we will have no choice but to install a bolt.

North Entrance

The mouth of the cave is approximately 6 x 2 meters in size and is oriented north to south. To access it, a bolt must be installed in the rock, also securing the rope a second time on a natural anchor in the rock that must be found, and if that is not possible, the best would be to install a second bolt.

A little more than a meter from the mouth of the chasm, we will find a step that can also be accessed without a rope, from which we will begin a rappel of approximately 11 meters that will place us on a kind of ledge formed by rocks, some of great size, that have been falling from the outside and that cover most of the crack.

At the north end of this ledge, a hole seems to indicate the continuation of the cave, but it is actually at the south end where it continues, once a rebelay is installed to continue the descent using a natural anchor. As we continue descending little by little, the natural light disappears until reaching total darkness that the headlamp takes care of illuminating, and throughout the entire route one can observe that the theoretical wall where we rest our feet is formed by threatening rocks fallen from the outside that have gradually been blocking a large part of the chasm's crack. After descending a little less than 32 meters from the previously mentioned ledge and 45 meters from the outside, we will find ourselves at the top of an enormous cone of debris that marks the beginning of the horizontal section of the chasm.

South Entrance

A rope of approximately 50 m will be necessary to descend through this entrance, where at about four meters depth we will pass next to a bridge that separates us from the other entrance, and at about ten meters we will need to install a rebelay to then let ourselves drop to the main chamber, just two or three meters from the other entrance, on top of the debris cone.

Floor plan

The floor plan of the chasm consists of a main chamber divided in two by a stalagmitic barrier, the larger part being the one containing the debris cone, which reaches a length of eighteen meters by as many meters wide.

We immediately realize that we are in a special place where for a long time water has done its work, as the chamber, with a height of fifty meters, is concretioned from base to ceiling where bats flutter about, frightened by our presence.

The slope of this chamber is considerable since the accumulation of rocks and animals fallen from the outside have been filling the floor.

Right at the deepest part of the chamber, in a beautiful corner and to the left as we descend, lie the remains of a Myotragus balearicus, also surrounded nearby by many other bones of different animals.

To the right of the Myotragus balearicus remains are some layers of strata that possibly have something to say about the history of the chasm, and a little further to the right the cave continues through a narrow crack that descends nine meters accessible without vertical progression equipment and at least another seven meters in which it is impossible to advance due to its narrowness.

A great stalagmitic wall separates this chamber from another parallel one, with a precious and impressive column standing out at the upper end of the two chambers.

A kind of balcony on which one can contemplate small stalagmites growing at its base is found above our heads at about eight meters altitude, impossible to access without climbing equipment.

The parallel chamber, accessed as soon as one arrives at the apex of the debris cone from outside, is a little smaller but equally impressive as the previous one. It is accessed through a kind of wide crack formed on its left side by the previously mentioned column and on the right by one end of the chamber. Noteworthy in this place is the great quantity of large blocks that have fallen on the floor, but in this case they are not rocks but pieces of speleothems.

The left side wall is what divides this chamber from the main one, while the right side wall shows us an impressive flowstone produced by an ancient water cascade now long gone; the front wall also has little to envy. Noteworthy is a stalagmite found more or less in the center of the chamber that, starting from a small base, as it grew vertically also grew horizontally, creating one of the most beautiful formations I have seen to this day.

Also, just a little before positioning ourselves at the apex of the debris cone, by climbing we will access a small and charming chamber whose mouth opens to the main chamber, with dimensions of about five meters by three meters wide, and inside which we will not be able to stand upright but where we will enjoy its charm, featuring extremely thin columns, stalagmites, and soda straws.

Fauna

While on the first time we accessed the chasm we did not find any bat specimens, on this second visit we did find a colony of between twenty and twenty-five large bats that I want to think are horseshoe bats since they were double or triple the size of those I had found until then in other cavities.

Many spiders can be observed as well as mosquitoes also of dimensions not commonly found outdoors in Mallorca, not to mention — since it personally surprised me — the occasional blowfly. Logically, any other type of fauna that the cavity may harbor is beyond my understanding and I leave it for specialized persons to describe.

For further reading:

JORDI SERRACOBO, BLANCA AMENGUAL, MARC LÓPEZ ROIG, JOSEP MÁRQUEZ, XAVIER BAYER, CISCO GUASCH, ANTONIA SÁNCHEZ i JOAN ANTONI OLIVER. (2007): Quinze anys d'estudis quiropterológics a les Illes Balears. Endins, 31: 125-139. Palma de Mallorca.

JORDANA, R., ET AL.: Descripción de una nueva especie de ENTOMOBRYA (COLLEMBOLA, ENTOMOBRYIDAE) de una cueva de Mallorca (Islas Baleares, España). Serie Zoologica, 29(2): 8-21. 2005.

ENCINAS, J.A., 1974. Inventario bio-espeleológico de Baleares, año 1973. Com. IV Simp. Bioespeleología. E.C.E., G.E. Pedraforca: 49-62. Barcelona.

GINÉS, A., 1982. Inventario de especies cavernícolas de las Islas Baleares. Endins, 9: 57-75.
